title: Capstone & Roadmap
subtitle: Twenty-one days of skills, one real app, and a map for everywhere you go next.
goal: Look back at the whole 22-day journey, build a complete Library app that combines classes, an ArrayList, a HashMap, methods, loops and conditionals, then plan your real Java future.

blocks.heading: You made it. 🏆
blocks.html:
Twenty-two days ago you printed your very first line of Java. Today you are going to build a whole little app from scratch — and it will use almost everything you learned. Take a breath and feel proud: you stuck with it.

This last day has three parts: a quick look back at the journey, one capstone project we build together piece by piece, and a roadmap for what to learn next. Let's finish strong.

blocks.heading: Everything you learned 📚
blocks.html:
Here is the whole adventure in one list. Each of these is a real superpower you now have:

  • Days 1-4: printing, variables and types, strings, and math operators.
  • Days 5-8: input, if conditionals, switch, and loops.
  • Days 9-10: arrays for holding many values, and methods to package reusable steps.
  • Days 11-15: the heart of Java — classes & objects, encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and interfaces.
  • Day 16: exceptions, so your program stays calm when things go wrong.
  • Days 17-19: the collections toolbox — ArrayList, HashMap, sets, and generics.
  • Days 20-21: modern Java — enums, records, lambdas, and streams.

That is a serious toolkit. Now we put it to work.

The same-file rule, one last time. Every runnable program has one public class Main with main, saved as Main.java. Helper classes live in the same file without the word public. Run it all with java Main.java.

blocks.heading: Our capstone: a Library app 🏛️
blocks.html:
We are going to build a little Library. People can borrow books and return them, and the library can report how many are out. We will build it in three steps so nothing feels scary.

Step 1: design a Book blueprint. Every book remembers its title, its author, and whether it is currently checked out.

blocks.caption: Step 1 - the Book blueprint (a class with fields)
blocks.output:
Matilda by Roald Dahl
Checked out? false
blocks.explain: Book is our blueprint (Day 11). It has three fields and a constructor that fills in the title and author and starts isOut at false, because a brand new book has not been borrowed yet. One object, one book.

blocks.heading: Step 2: a Library that holds many books
blocks.html: One book is not a library. We need a list of them. That is exactly what an ArrayList is for (Day 17). Our Library class will keep an ArrayList<Book>, add books to it with a method, and loop over them to print a list.

blocks.caption: Step 2 - a Library holding an ArrayList of books
blocks.output:
The library has 3 books:
- Matilda by Roald Dahl (available)
- Holes by Louis Sachar (available)
- Wonder by R.J. Palacio (available)
blocks.explain: The Library keeps an ArrayList<Book>. addBook builds a Book and adds it. listBooks uses a for-each loop (Day 8) to walk every book, and the ? : trick picks the word out or available. Three classes, one public Main.

blocks.heading: Step 3: borrowing and returning
blocks.html: Now the real magic. We add a HashMap (Day 18) that remembers who borrowed each book — the title is the key, the person is the value. The borrow method uses conditionals (Day 6) to handle three cases: the book does not exist, it is already out, or it is free to take.

blocks.caption: Step 3 - the full Library app (classes + ArrayList + HashMap + methods + loop + conditionals)
blocks.output:
Sam borrowed Matilda
Ava borrowed Holes
Matilda is already borrowed by Sam
Holes was returned
Books out: 1 of 3
blocks.explain: This one program uses almost everything: classes and objects, an ArrayList of books, a HashMap of who-has-what, methods like find and borrow, a for-each loop, and if / else if / else conditionals. Sam takes Matilda, Ava takes Holes, Leo is told Matilda is taken, Holes comes back, and the report counts one book still out. You just built a real app.

blocks.question: In the Library app, why did we use a HashMap<String, String> for borrowedBy?
options: To remember which person (value) borrowed each book title (key)
To make the program run faster than a loop
blocks.explain: A HashMap maps a key to a value. Here the book title is the key and the borrower's name is the value, so we can instantly look up who has a given book.

blocks.question: When borrow is called for a book that is already out, which branch runs?
blocks.explain: The book exists, so b is not null. It is already checked out, so b.isOut is true and the else if branch prints that it is already borrowed.

blocks.html: Big programs are just small steps stacked up. We did not write the whole Library at once — we built a Book, then a list of books, then borrowing. Break every big idea into tiny steps and you can build anything.

blocks.title: Give Book a checkOut method
blocks.instructions: The Book class has a field isOut that starts false. Add a method checkOut() that sets this.isOut to true. The Main code already builds a book and calls b.checkOut(), then prints whether it is out.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — a method that changes the object's own state. That is OOP in action!
blocks.failMsg: Add void checkOut() inside Book and have it run this.isOut = true;.

blocks.title: List books on a shelf
blocks.instructions: Make an ArrayList<String> called shelf, add the three titles "Matilda", "Holes", and "Wonder", print how many books are on the shelf, then loop over the shelf printing each title with a "- " in front.
blocks.solutionOutput:
Shelf has 3 books
- Matilda
- Holes
- Wonder
blocks.passMsg: An ArrayList plus a loop — two tools working together. You've got this.
blocks.failMsg: Use shelf.add(...) three times, print shelf.size(), then a for-each loop over shelf.

blocks.heading: Where to go next 🗺️
blocks.html:
You have outgrown the browser. Here is your roadmap to becoming a real-world Java developer:

  1. Install a real JDK. Download a free, long-term JDK from Adoptium / Temurin (adoptium.net). That gives you the java and javac commands on your own computer.
  2. Pick an IDE. An IDE is a smart code editor. Try IntelliJ IDEA (the Community edition is free) or VS Code with the Java extension pack. They autocomplete, catch mistakes, and run your code with one click.
  3. Learn a build tool. Real projects use many files and libraries. Maven or Gradle handle that for you, downloading libraries and building your app automatically.
  4. Then pick a path by what excites you:
    • Android apps - build phone apps (learn a little Kotlin too; it pairs perfectly with Java).
    • Backend & websites - learn Spring Boot to build the servers that power apps and sites.
    • Desktop apps - use JavaFX to make windowed programs with buttons and graphics.
    • Data - process and analyze big piles of information with Java's data libraries.

You are a Java programmer now. You understand variables, loops, classes, objects, collections, and streams. Everything from here is just more of the same building blocks, stacked higher. Keep building small projects, stay curious, and don't be afraid to break things — that is how everyone learns. Go make something awesome. 🚀

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You're a project manager tracking tasks. Each task has a title, a status, and a priority. Build a mini task planner that adds tasks, marks them done, and prints a progress report.
blocks.caption: Project task planner
blocks.output:
✓ [HIGH] Design database
✓ [HIGH] Write API endpoints
○ [MEDIUM] Add unit tests
○ [LOW] Write README
Progress: 2/4 done
blocks.explain: This final example combines ArrayList (Day 17), HashMap (Day 18), methods (Day 10), loops (Day 8), and conditionals (Day 6) — exactly the skills you've built over 22 days. A real project planner, no libraries needed.

blocks.title: Final Exam — Full Course Review
blocks.intro: The ultimate checkpoint! 5 questions covering everything from Day 1 to Day 22. 70% to pass.

questions.question: What does the keyword 'static' mean on a method?
options: The method belongs to the class, not to an object instance
questions.explain: A static method belongs to the class itself, so you can call it without creating an object. That's why main is static. (Day 10)

questions.question: In OOP, what is encapsulation?
options: Making fields private and controlling access through methods
Writing many classes in one file
questions.explain: Encapsulation means hiding internal data (private fields) and exposing it through controlled getters/setters. (Day 12)

questions.question: What does a try/catch block do?
options: Runs risky code and handles errors if they occur
questions.explain: try attempts the risky code; if an exception is thrown, catch handles it gracefully instead of crashing. (Day 16)

questions.question: What keyword does a class use to fulfill an interface's contract?
questions.explain: A class uses 'implements' to promise it will provide all methods listed in the interface. (Day 15)

questions.question: Which modern Java feature creates an immutable data class in one line?
questions.explain: A record (Java 16+) auto-generates constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString. It's immutable by design. (Day 20)

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You're a project manager tracking tasks. Each task has a title, status, and priority. Build a mini task planner that adds tasks, marks them done, and prints a progress report.
blocks.caption: Project task planner
blocks.output:
v [HIGH] Design database
v [HIGH] Write API endpoints
o [MEDIUM] Add unit tests
o [LOW] Write README
Progress: 2/4 done
blocks.explain: This final example combines ArrayList (Day 17), HashMap (Day 18), methods (Day 10), loops (Day 8), and conditionals (Day 6).

blocks.title: Final Exam — Full Course Review
blocks.intro: The ultimate checkpoint! 5 questions covering Days 1–22. 70% to pass.

questions.question: What does the keyword 'static' mean on a method?
options: The method belongs to the class, not to an object instance
questions.explain: A static method belongs to the class itself — callable without creating an object. (Day 10)

questions.question: In OOP, what is encapsulation?
options: Making fields private and controlling access through methods
Writing many classes in one file
questions.explain: Encapsulation = hiding internal data (private fields) + controlled getters/setters. (Day 12)

questions.question: What does a try/catch block do?
options: Runs risky code and handles errors if they occur
questions.explain: try attempts risky code; catch handles exceptions gracefully. (Day 16)

questions.question: What keyword does a class use to fulfill an interface's contract?
questions.explain: A class uses 'implements' to promise it provides all interface methods. (Day 15)

questions.question: Which modern Java feature creates an immutable data class in one line?
questions.explain: A record auto-generates constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString. Immutable by design. (Day 20)

challenge.title: Build a Quiz app from scratch
challenge.instructions: Combine everything you learned into one app. Build a Question class with two fields, prompt and correct, plus a constructor. Build a Quiz class that holds an ArrayList<Question> and an int score starting at 0. Give Quiz these methods: addQuestion(prompt, correct) which adds a new Question; find(prompt) which loops to return the matching Question or null; answer(prompt, given) which finds the question and uses if / else if / else to print 'No such question', 'Correct: ' + prompt and add 1 to score, or 'Wrong: ' + prompt; and report() which prints 'Score: ' + score + ' of ' + questions.size(). In main, add the three questions shown, answer them as shown, then call report().
challenge.solutionOutput:
Correct: Capital of Japan?
Wrong: 2 + 2 equals?
Correct: Color of the sky?
Score: 2 of 3
challenge.hint: Question is just a tiny blueprint with two fields. Quiz holds an ArrayList of them plus a score. In answer, first call find(prompt); if it is null print 'No such question', else if q.correct.equals(given) add to score and print Correct, otherwise print Wrong. To extend it: add a method that prints all the questions, or one that resets the score to 0!
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built a complete app using classes, an ArrayList, methods, a loop, and conditionals — all on your own. That is 22 days of Java, finished. You are a Java programmer now. Go build something amazing! 🚀
